one_line_takeaway: Adding Gaussian noise (to either raw positions or control inputs, then averaging N=20 predictions) consistently lowers ADE degradation from kinematically-constrained PGD attacks on Trajectron++/ADAPT across L-GAP and rounD, at little to no cost on clean data, but the best smoothing variant and noise level are model-dependent and the approach remains empirical rather than certified.
key_finding: Randomized smoothing consistently reduces ADE under adversarial attack (dmax>0) relative to the unsmoothed base model across all four dataset/model combinations, while causing only marginal (sometimes zero or negative, i.e. improved) degradation on unperturbed data (dmax=0), except for Trajectron++ on rounD where clean-data ADE clearly worsens under smoothing (Table III: 0.116 m unsmoothed vs. 0.300-0.916 m smoothed). Concrete numbers: on L-GAP with ADAPT (Table II), unsmoothed ADE rises from 1.169 m (clean) to 2.952 m at dmax=1.0 m, whereas control-based smoothing at sigma=0.25 achieves 1.164 m (clean) and 1.428 m at dmax=1.0 m. The paper highlights that smoothing reduces the sensitivity of ADE to attack magnitude dmax: for ADAPT with ctrl smoothing at sigma=0.25, the ADE gap between dmax=0.25 and dmax=1.0 shrinks to -0.81 m on L-GAP and 0.028 m to 0.92 m on rounD, versus much larger gaps for the unsmoothed model. Which smoothing variant works best is model-dependent: ADAPT does best with control-based smoothing (Tables II, IV) while Trajectron++ (in eval-only mode) generally does best with position-based smoothing. Lower noise levels (sigma=0.25) most often give the smallest ADE. A single robust configuration is identified per model that consistently improves over baseline across both datasets for dmax>0: position-based smoothing at sigma=0.25 for Trajectron++, and control-based smoothing at sigma=0.25 for ADAPT.
core_contribution: The paper proposes and empirically evaluates randomized smoothing as a defense against adversarial attacks on trajectory prediction models for autonomous driving. It introduces two concrete smoothing variants for this setting: (1) 'position-based' smoothing, which adds Gaussian noise directly to the position coordinates of an agent's past observed states before feeding them to the base predictor and averaging N=20 sampled outputs; and (2) 'control-based' smoothing, which instead perturbs the underlying control inputs (via an assumed dynamical model mapping controls to positions/velocities), with the noise variance calibrated (e.g. sigma_a = sigma*sqrt(3)/sqrt(H^3 * dt^2) for acceleration) so accumulated positional noise roughly matches the position-based approach. The authors test both approaches, evaluated either only at test time ('eval') or with smoothing noise injected during training as well ('train & eval'), across two base predictors (Trajectron++, ADAPT) and two datasets (L-GAP, rounD), against kinematically-constrained white-box PGD-style adversarial attacks from prior work (Schumann et al., ref [22]) at three perturbation budgets (dmax in {0.25, 0.5, 1.0} m) and three smoothing noise levels (sigma in {0.25, 0.5, 1.0} m).
threat_model: White-box adversarial attacker perturbing the past observed trajectory (positions/kinematic states) of a single 'adversarial' target agent, using the kinematically-constrained projected-gradient-descent attack from Schumann et al. [22] (Eq. 1 in this paper), which iteratively adds a gradient step on the average displacement error (ADE) loss and projects the perturbation onto a feasible set (hypercube around the unperturbed input) to keep the attack physically realistic. Perturbation magnitude is bounded by dmax in {0.25, 0.5, 1.0} meters. The defender is the trajectory-prediction model deployed in an autonomous vehicle; the goal is to keep ADE low despite this bounded, kinematically-plausible perturbation of another road agent's observed past trajectory. This is an empirical/heuristic robustness evaluation, not a certified-robustness paper -- the authors explicitly note certified guarantees (from Rekavandi et al. [32]) are left to future work.
limitations: Explicitly noted by the authors: (1) the study is empirical only -- it does not establish certified robustness guarantees, and future work is needed to check whether the probabilistic guarantees from Rekavandi et al.'s alpha-smoothing framework [32] produce tight, useful prediction sets for trajectory prediction; (2) the ideal smoothing approach (position- vs. control-based) is model-dependent with 'no clear preference,' and the paper suggests combining both as future work; (3) the calibration matching noise variance across the two smoothing approaches (footnote 4) is an approximation whose comparability 'cannot be guaranteed'; (4) the evaluation covers only two base models, two datasets, and one attack method (Schumann et al. [22]), and the authors explicitly state that 'expanding to more models, datasets, and adversarial attack methods would improve the support for the claims'; (5) Trajectron++ on rounD is an outlier where smoothing degrades clean-data performance, which the paper reports without a definitive explanation; (6) the attack considered is a single-agent, white-box PGD attack -- other attack families (e.g., transfer/black-box attacks referenced in cited work [22],[23]) are not evaluated against the proposed defense.

datasets_used: L-GAP (driving-simulator left-turn gap-acceptance dataset, augmented with nuScenes for training)
rounD (real-world drone-collected roundabout traffic dataset from German roads)
quantitative_results: L-GAP/Trajectron++ (Table I): no-smoothing ADE 0.473 m (clean) rising to 2.057 m at dmax=1.0 m
L-GAP/ADAPT (Table II): no-smoothing ADE 1.169 m (clean) rising to 2.952 m at dmax=1.0 m; control-based smoothing sigma=0.25 gives 1.164 m (clean) and 1.428 m at dmax=1.0 m
rounD/Trajectron++ (Table III): no-smoothing ADE 0.116 m (clean) rising sharply to 6.335 m at dmax=1.0 m; train&eval control-based smoothing sigma=0.25 gives 0.376 m (clean) and 1.104 m at dmax=1.0 m

rounD/ADAPT (Table IV): no-smoothing ADE 0.537 m (clean) rising to 0.747 m at dmax=1.0 m; eval-only control-based smoothing sigma=0.25 gives 0.494 m (clean, an improvement over baseline) and 0.569 m at dmax=1.0 m

ADE sensitivity-to-attack-magnitude reduction example (ADAPT, ctrl, sigma=0.25): dmax=0.25-to-1.0 ADE gap shrinks to -0.81 m (L-GAP) and 0.028-to-0.92 m (rounD), versus much larger gaps for the unsmoothed baseline
N=20 samples used for smoothed prediction averaging in all evaluation experiments
baselines_compared: Unsmoothed (no-smoothing) Trajectron++ and ADAPT base predictors, evaluated under the same adversarial attacks and perturbation budgets as the smoothed variants
